title: PRESENTATION X COHOMOLOGICAL DIMENSION: FIRST RESULTS

pages.title: PRESENTATION X COHOMOLOGICAL DIMENSION: FIRST RESULTS

blocks.html:
PRESENTATION X
COHOMOLOGICAL DIMENSION: FIRST RESULTSby M. Artin
Summary

1. Introduction
2. Auxiliary results for a body
3. Field of fractions of a strictly local ring
4. Cohomological dimension: case \(\ell\) invertible in \(\mathcal{O}_X\)
5. Cohomological dimension: case \(\ell = p\).
6. Cohomological dimension for a finite type prescheme on Spec \(\mathbb{Z}\).
1. Introduction
In this presentation we agree, unless expressly stated to the contrary, that "fasceau" means "abelian beam".

Let \(X\) be a schema and \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\). “Let’s remember” what we call \(\ell\)-cohomological dimension of \(X\) the largest integer \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell X = n\) (or \(= \infty\) if this number does not exist) such that \(H^n(X,F) \neq 0\) for at least a beam of \(\ell\)-twist \(F\) on \(X\). If \(X = \operatorname{Spec} A\), we write \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell A = \operatorname{cd}_\ell \operatorname{Spec} A\). Taking into account the dictionary (VIII 2) we find thus the "classical" notion of \([\mathrm{CG}]\) in the case where \(A = k\) is a body. As we obviously have the formula \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell \left( \coprod_i X_i \right) = \sup_i \operatorname{cd}_\ell X_i \] we can, obviously, extend the classical theory to a ring any artinian. This theory is presented in \((\mathrm{CG}) = \) "cohomology Galoisian" by Serre. We will provide some details in the No. 2.

Take for example the case of a variety \(X\) algebraic irre- deducible, dimension \(n\), on a separably closed body \(k\), and fi- xons \(\ell \neq \operatorname{car} k\). A theorem that we will find in No. 4 gives for the cohomological dimension the increase \(2n\), which is the “true” dimension table" of \(X\) (suggested by the topological analogy in the case where \(k = \mathbb{C}\)). This result is quite elementary from the theory for the bodies, and in fact the increase by \(2n\) can be improved except in case \(X\) is complete: there is another increase, \(2n-1\), which is true whenever in \(X\) at least one is missing period.” This last increase is the good "stable increase", in the sense that each \(X\) of dimension \(n\) contains an open of dimension coho- mological \(2n-1\). A third increase is obtained for a variety affine, where we obtain \(n\) as a cohomological dimension. It's a gene- realization of one of Lefschetz's theorems for hyperplane sections. These last two assertions are deeper and cannot be demonstrated only later (XIV).

Note that the cohomological dimension is not a notion local (unlike what happens in paracompact spaces). For example, a Henselian ring with a separably closed residual body is of zero cohomological dimension, but if we remove the closed point, we can obtain an arbitrarily large number, namely \(2n-1\) if \(n = \dim A\) in the most important cases; which is also in agreement with the topological analogy provided by the cohomology of \(U - \{x\}\), where \(U\) is a small open neighborhood of a point \(x\) of an analytical space complex.
2. Auxiliary results on a body
We have the next one

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let \(k'/k\) be a finite type extension of a body \(k\), and either \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\). So \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell k' \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell k + \operatorname{deg.tr.}(k'/k) , \] If the strict inequality is true and if \(\ell \neq \operatorname{car} k\), then \(\ell = 2\), \(k\) is orderable, and \(k'\) is not orderable (i.e. \(-1\) is a sum of squares in \(k'\)).

Recall that when \(\ell = \operatorname{car} k\) we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k \leqslant 1\) (CG II 2.2) therefore the question of equality in 2.1 hardly arises. Furthermore, we have \(\operatorname{cd}_2 k = \infty\) if \(k\) is orderable. In fact the closure algebraic \(\bar{k}\) of \(k\) contains a subextension \(\tilde{k}\) (a subfield ordered maximum of \(\bar{k}\)) with \([\bar{k}:\tilde{k}] = 2\). Obviously, \(\operatorname{cd}_2 \tilde{k} = \infty\), and \(\operatorname{cd}_2 \tilde{k} \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_2 k\) according to (CG I Prop. 14).

Demonstration. It's almost all well known. Inequality as well as equality if \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k' < \infty\), are Tate results (CG II 4.1.2). It remains to demonstrate that \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k' < \infty\) and \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k = \infty\) implies \(k\) orderable and \(\ell = 2\), and it suffices to handle both cases following:

1. a) \([k':k] < \infty\),
  2. b) \(k' = k(x)\) with \(x\) transcending on \(k\).

In case a) we can assume \(k'/k\) separable. So the group of Galois \(H = G(\bar{k}/k')\) is an open subgroup of \(G = G(\bar{k}/k)\) and according to a result of Serre [5] we have \(\operatorname{cd} H = \operatorname{cd} G\) unless \(G\) contains an order element \(\ell\). Like an algebraically closed body \(\bar{k}\) cannot have a subfield \(\tilde{k}\) of finite index unless \(\ell = 2\) and \(\tilde{k}\) is orderable [7], we find the result.

Let's deal with case b). Let \(R\) be the henselized of \(\operatorname{Spec} k[x]\) at point \(x = 0\), which is a discrete valuation ring, and let \(K\) be the body of fractions of \(R\). Then \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k(x) < \infty\) implies \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell K < \infty\). He It is therefore sufficient to demonstrate that \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell K < \infty\) implies \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k < \infty\), which is a consequence of the following theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.2(∗)

Let \(R\) be a Henselian discrete valuation ring, with fraction body \(K\) and residual body \(k\). Or \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\). We have \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell K = \operatorname{cd}_\ell k + 1 \] in the following two situations:

  1. (i) \(\ell \neq \operatorname{car} k\).
  2. (ii) \(\ell = \operatorname{car} K\), \(k\) perfect, and \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k < \infty\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note

Naturally, if \(\ell = \operatorname{car} K\) we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell K \leqslant 1\) in any case (CG II 2.2). It therefore remains to study the relationship of \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell K\) with, perhaps, the absolute differentials module \(\Omega^1_k\) (EGA IV 20.4.3), in the case of unequal characteristics when \(\operatorname{car} k = \ell\), \(k\) not perfect.

Proof of the theorem. As for theorem (2.1), the Most of it is due to Tate. Case (ii) is dealt with explicitly in (CG II Prop. 12) if \(R\) is complete, and we reduce ourselves to this case by means of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2.1

Let \(R\) be a Henselian discrete valuation ring, with body of fractions \(K\), and let \(\hat{K}\) be the body of fractions of the completed \(\hat{R}\) from \(R\). So \[ G(\overline{\hat{K}}/\hat{K}) \simeq G(\overline{K}/K) . \]

Demonstration. This means that the functor \(L \longmapsto \hat{L} = L \otimes_K \hat{K}\) from the category of \(K\)-even algebras in the category of \(\hat{K}\)-algebras etales is an equivalence. Now each equated algebra \(\hat{K}'\) of \(\hat{K}\) is induced by a suitable equal algebra \(K'\) of \(K\). Indeed, if \(\hat{K}' = \hat{K}[\alpha]\) where \(\alpha\) satisfies the irreducible separable equation \(f(T) = 0\) to coefficients in \(\hat{R}\), just take for \(K'\) the algebra \(K[\alpha_0]\), where \(\alpha_0\) is the root of an equation \(f_0(T) = 0\) with coefficients in \(R\) with \(f_0 \equiv f \ (\hat{\mathfrak{m}}^N)\), \(N\) large and \(\hat{\mathfrak{m}}\) the maximum ideal of \(\hat{R}\). This is the application of the usual “Newton method” (Bourbaki, Alg. Comm. III 4.5). It remains to prove that the functor is full- faithfully (he is obviously faithful), and it amounts to the same thing to demonstrate clear that if \(K'\) is a body, it is the same for \(\hat{K}'\). Let \(R'\) be normalized from \(R\) into \(K'\), which is finite on \(R\) since \(K'/K\) is se- parable. The ring \(R'\) is a discrete valuation ring, and the supplemented with \(R'\) is \(\hat{R}' = R' \otimes_R \hat{R}\). So \(\hat{R}'\) is a ring of valua- discrete tion, and since \(\hat{K}'\) is the total ring of fractions, it is indeed a body.

Let us deal with assertion i) of 2.2. If \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k < \infty\) we is further reduced by 2.2.1 to (CG II Prop. 12) (the hypothesis made in the statement of this proposition that \(k\) is perfect is not used in the demonstration only in the case of residual characteristic \(\ell\)). It therefore remains to demonstrate that \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell K < \infty\) implies \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k < \infty\).

Let \(\overline{G} = G(\bar{k}/k) = G(\widetilde{K}/K)\) where \(\widetilde{K}\) is the unbranched extension maximum of \(K\) (i.e. the body of fractions of the valuation ring discrete \(\widetilde{R}\), with separably closed residual body, strict henselized of \(R\)), \(G = G(\overline{K}/K)\), \(H = G(\overline{K}/\widetilde{K})\), therefore \(\overline{G} = G/H\). Let \(\overline{G}' \subset \overline{G}\) be a \(\ell\)-subgroup of Sylow, and let \(k'/k\), \(K'/K\) be the corresponding extensions laying at \(\overline{G}'\). We have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k < \infty\) if and only if \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k' < \infty\) (CG I Prop. 14), therefore by replacing \(R\) by its normalized in \(K'\), we reduces to the case where \(\overline{G}\) is a \(\ell\)-group. It is then sufficient to demonstrate that if \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell G < \infty\), then \(H^N(\overline{G}, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\) for \(N\) large enough (CG I Prop. 20).
We have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell H = 1\) according to the part of i) already demonstrated, applied to \(\widetilde{R}\). In addition, we have
(2.2.2) \[ H^1(H, \mu_\ell) = H^1(\operatorname{Spec} \widetilde{K}, \mu_\ell) = \widetilde{K}^*/\widetilde{K}^{*\ell} = \mathbb{Z}/\ell . \]

Indeed, \(H^1(\operatorname{Spec} \widetilde{K}, \mu_\ell) = \widetilde{K}^*/\widetilde{K}^{*\ell}\) according to Kummer's theory IX 3.2. We have an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \widetilde{R}^* \longrightarrow \widetilde{K}^* \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z} \longrightarrow 0 , \] and \(\widetilde{R}^*\) is divisible by \(\ell\) since \(\widetilde{R}\) is strictly local and \(\ell\) invertible in \(\widetilde{R}\), hence (2.2.2).
Consider the Hochschild-Serre spectral sequence \[ E_2^{pq} = H^p(\overline{G}, H^q(H, \mu_\ell)) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(G, \mu_\ell) . \]

We have \(E_2^{pq} = 0\) if \(q > 1\). If in addition \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell G < \infty\), the morphism "cobord" \[ H^{n-1}(\overline{G}, H^1(H, \mu_\ell)) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(\overline{G}, \mu_\ell) \] must be bijective for \(n\) large enough, and it must be shown that then \[ H^{n-1}(\overline{G}, H^1(H, \mu_\ell)) = H^{n-1}(\overline{G}, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 . \]

Let's set \(R' = R[x']\) with \(x'^\ell = x\), \(x\) the local parameter of \(R\), and let \(K'\) be the field of fractions of \(R'\), \(G' = G(\overline{K}/K')\), etc... The residual field of \(R'\) is \(k\), since we assumed \(\overline{G}\) a \(\ell\)-group, therefore that the roots \(\ell\).ths of the unit are in \(K\). We have a diagram \[ \xymatrix{ H' \ar[r] \ar[d] & G' \ar[r] \ar[d] & \overline{G} \ar@{=}[d] \\ H \ar[r] & G \ar[r] & \overline{G} } \] hence a morphism of Hochschild-Serre spectral sequences, which gives a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=3em{ H^{n-1}(\overline{G}, H^1(H', \mu_\ell)) \ar[dr] & \\ & H^{n+1}(\overline{G}, \mu_\ell) \\ H^{n-1}(\overline{G}, H^1(H, \mu_\ell)) \ar[uu]^{\varepsilon} \ar[ur] & } \] As \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell G < \infty\) implies \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell G' < \infty\), all morphisms in this diagram are isomorphisms for \(n\) large. But the morphism \[ \mathbb{Z}/\ell = H^1(H, \mu_\ell) \longrightarrow H^1(H', \mu_\ell) = \mathbb{Z}/\ell \] which induces the arrow \(\varepsilon\) is obviously zero, so \(H^{n-1}(\overline{G}, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.3

Let \(A\) be a Noetherian Henselian ring of dimension 1, and \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\) invertible into \(A\). Let \(k\) be the residual body of \(A\) and \(R(A)\) the ring of rational functions on \(\operatorname{Spec} A\). We have \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell k + 1 , \] and the equality is true if \(\ell \neq 2\) or if \(k\) is not orderable.

Demonstration. By replacing \(A\) with \(A/P\) for an ideal pre- minimum \(P\) of \(A\) and by applying (VIII 1) we see that we can assume \(A\) integrity. Let \(K\) be its field of fractions, \(R\) its norma- lized, which is local and Henselian, therefore a ring of discrete valuation (he is Noetherian according to Krull-Akizuki (Bourbaki, Alg. Comm. Chap. 7, § 2, prop. 5)) and let \(k'\) be the residual body of \(R\). It is known that \([k':k] < \infty\) (loc. cit.). Now \(K\) is the field of fractions of \(R\) and we therefore have \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell K = \operatorname{cd}_\ell k' + 1 \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell k + 1 \] according to 2.2, with equality under the last hypothesis of 2.3, according to 2.1.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example

Equality is not true for \(\ell = 2\), \(A\) being the henselized at the origin of \(\mathbb{R}[x,y]/(x^2+y^2)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

Let \(A\) be a Noetherian local ring with a residual body \(k\), and let \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\) be invertible in \(A\). Suppose \(\ell \neq 2\) where that no residual body of \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) is orderable. So we have \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) \geqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell k + \dim A , \] where \(R(A)\) is the ring of rational functions on \(\operatorname{Spec} A\).

Demonstration. We can assume \(A\) to be intact, with the body of fractions \(R(A) = K\). Let \(x \in \mathfrak{r}A\) be an element which is not a divisor from zero. Then \(\dim A/(x) = \dim A - 1\). By induction on \(\dim A\), we have \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A/(x)) \geqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell k + \dim A - 1 . \] Let \(k'\) be a residual body of \(R(A/(x))\) such that \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k' = \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A/(x))\) (cf VIII 1), and let \(P\) be the prime ideal, core of \(A \longrightarrow k'\). The ring \(A_P\) is of dimension 1. It is therefore sufficient to demonstrate the corollary for the an- local network \(A_P\), that is, we are reduced to the case where \(\dim A = 1\). Let \(\widetilde{A}\) be the henselized of \(A\). Each residual body of \(R(\widetilde{A})\) identifies itself to a separable extension of \(R(\widetilde{A}) = K\), and we therefore have \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) = \operatorname{cd}_\ell K \geqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(\widetilde{A}) . \] We are thus brought back to the case where \(A\) is of dimension 1 and Henselian, this which is a consequence of 2.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian schema and let \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\) invertible to \(X\). So \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) \geqslant \dim X . \]

Indeed, let \(x \in X\) be a point such that \(\dim X = \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) (or such that \(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is very large if \(\dim X = \infty\)). Let \(A\) be strict henselized of \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\). So we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) \geqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}) \geqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A)\) according to the usual reasoning. Additionally, \(\dim A = \dim X\) (resp. is very large). If \(\ell = 2\), and if \(\ell\) is invertible on \(X\), then the equation \(T^2+ 1 = 0\) is separable on \(A\), so \(-1\) is a square in \(A\) because that \(A\) is strictly local, so the residual bodies of \(A\) are not orderable. So the assumptions of 2.4 are satisfied for \(A\) in in any case, and we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) \geqslant \dim A\), hence the result.

footnotes.html: (∗) This theorem was recently proven by Ax [1].

pages.title: 3. Field of fractions of a strictly local ring

blocks.html: 3. Field of fractions of a strictly local ring

We will have an essential need, for the following issues, from the knowledge of \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A)\), where \(A\) is a strictly local ring, that is to say Henselian with a separably closed residual body. Unfortunate- ment we do not know \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A)\) in the general case, for example if \(A = \hat{\mathbb{Z}}_p[[x]]\) \((p \neq \ell)\).
We conjecture, however, that if \(A\) is Noetherian and \(\ell\) is invertible in \(A\), we will have
(3.1) \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) = \dim A \qquad (\ell \text{ inversible dans } A) , \]
at least in the most important cases, for example if \(A\) is a year- excellent water (EGA IV 7.8.2).

Note that if \(\dim A = 1\), (3.1) is true according to 2.3. From moreover, we have the inequality \(\geqslant\) according to 2.5 in any case. We will demonstrate later (XIX) that (3.1) is also true if \(A\) is excellent of zero residual characteristic, using the resolution of the singulars lities [3]. Here we will prove (3.1) only in the easy case 3.2. below.

Naturally we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) \leqslant 1\) if \(A\) is of characteristic than \(\ell\), so this case is trivial. It also remains to analyze the case unequal characteristics, with residual characteristic \(\ell\), and we don't expect 3.1 to be true in this case. It will be necessary to tainly take into account the rank of the module \(\Omega^1_k\) (\(k\) the residual body), i.e. of the degree \([k:k^\ell]\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.2

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of finite type on the spectrum of a body \(k\) or on the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring \(R\), and let \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\) be invertible in \(k\) (resp. \(R\)). So 3.1 is true for each ring \(A\) which is a strict localization of \(X\).
Demonstration. Let us take \(X\) integrated (we reduce to this case as usually), and either \(n = \dim X\).

Suppose that \(X\) is defined on a separably closed field \(k\). Then for each ring \(A\) which is a strict localization of \(X\) in one closed point, we have \(\dim A = n\). Like every residual body \(K\) of \(R(A)\) identifies with an algebraic extension of \(R(X)\) and as \[ \operatorname{deg.tr.}(R(X)/k) = \dim X = n , \] we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell K \leqslant n\) according to 2.1, hence \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) \leqslant n\). The opposite inequality is a consequence of 2.5.

Now suppose that \(X\) is of finite type on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, and let \(A\) be the strict localization of \(X\) at a point \(x\). If \(x\) is not above the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), we immediately reduce to the case where \(X\) is of finite type on a body (by locating \(R\)). For the same reason, we can also assume that the morphism \(X \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} R\) is dominant.

Let us assume for the moment that furthermore \(R\) is a ring of discrete valuation strictly local and the point \(x\) is closed in \(X\). So we have \(\dim A = \dim X = n\) (EGA IV 5.6.5). Like every body residual \(K\) of \(R(A)\) identifies with a separable extension of \(R(X)\) we have \[ \begin{aligned} \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) &\leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell L + \operatorname{deg.tr.}(R(X)/L) \\ &= 1 + (n-1) = n \end{aligned} \] according to 2.1 and 2.2, where \(L\) is the field of fractions of \(R\), hence the result in this case, taking into account 2.5.
We deduce the general case from these particular cases by means of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

(i) Let \(X\) be of finite type on the spectrum of a body \(k\) and let \(A\) a strict localized ring of \(X\) at a point \(x\). So there is a separably closed body \(k'\), a schema \(X'\) of finite type on \(\operatorname{Spec} k'\), and a point \(x'\) closed in \(X'\) such that \(A\) is isomorphic to a ring strict localized from \(X'\) to \(x'\).

(ii) Let \(X\) of finite type on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), \(R\) be a valuation ring discrete, i.e. \(x\) a point of \(X\) above the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) and or \(A\) a strict localization of \(X\) in \(x\). There is a ring of valua- discrete strictly local \(R'\), a schema \(X'\) of finite type on \(\operatorname{Spec} R'\), and a closed point \(x'\) of \(X'\) above the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec} R'\), such that \(A\) is isomorphic to a strict localization of \(X'\) to \(x'\).

Demonstration. (i) We can take \(X\) affine. Let \(Y\) be the adhesion of \(x\) in \(X\), with the reduced induced structure, and either \(d = \dim Y\). By the normalization lemma ([2] Chap. V § 3 n° 1), there exists a morphism \[ X \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} k[y_1,\dots ,y_d] = T \] inducing a finite morphism \(Y \longrightarrow T\). Consequently the strict localized ring of \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is isomorphic to the strict localization of \(\mathcal{O}_{X',x'}\), where \(X' = X \times_T \operatorname{Spec} \overline{R}(T)\), and where \(x'\) is a closed point in \(X'\).

(ii) This case is treated in a similar way. We can assume \(X\) affine, ring \(A\). Let \(Y\) be the adhesion of \(x\) in \(X\) with the structure in- reduced pick, which is a closed subscheme of the closed fiber \(X_0\) of \(X\) on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), and either \(d = \dim Y\). So, we take note of the elements \(y_1,\dots,y_d\) of \(A \otimes_R k\) considered in i) in elements of \(A\), we find a morphism \[ X \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} R[y_1,\dots,y_d] = T \] inducing a finite morphism \(Y \longrightarrow T_0\). Let \(R'\) be the strict localization of \(R[y_1,\dots,y_d] = B\) into the prime ideal \(\underline{\mathfrak{m}}B\). Then \(R'\) is a ring of strictly local discrete valuation, and we can take \(X' = X \times_T \operatorname{Spec} R'\), and \(x'\) a closed point of \(X'\) above \(x\).
4. Cohomological dimension: case \(\ell\) invertible in \(\mathcal{O}_X\)

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian schema, \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\), and \(\varphi\) a function on \(X\), with values in \(\mathbb{N}\), and satisfying the following conditions:

(i) For each \(x \in X\), \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k(x) \leqslant \varphi(x)\).

(ii) Let \(x \in X\) and \(y \in Y = \) adhere to \(x\), \(y \neq x\). Let \(K\) be the ring rational functions of a strict localization of \(Y\) above \(y\). We have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell K < \varphi(x) - \varphi(y)\).

Then for each beam of \(\ell\)-torsion \(F\) on \(X\) we have \[ H^q(X,F) = 0 \] if \[ q > \varphi(F) \underset{\text{déf}}{=} \operatorname{Sup} \{ \varphi(x) \mid x \in X,\ F_{\bar{x}} \neq 0 \} . \]

Demonstration. The hypotheses will also be verified for the restriction function of \(\varphi\), to any sub-preschema of \(X\), therefore by Noetherian induction we can suppose the theorem true for each closed subpreschema of \(X\) distinct from \(X\). Let \(F\) be given and let's write \(F = \varinjlim F_i\) where the \(F_i\) are the constructible sub-sheaves of \(F\) (IX 2.9 (iii)).

For each \(F_i\), the set of \(x \in X\) such that \(F_x \neq 0\) is constructed tible in \(X\), and we can apply the recurrence hypothesis to each \(F_i\) whose support is not dense. We thus see that the hypothesis of recurrence actually implies that the theorem is true for every \(F\) which is zero at least one of the maximum points of \(X\). We will therefore assume that \(F\) does not satisfy this condition.

Now let \(i : \operatorname{Spec} R(X) \longrightarrow X\) be the inclusion, where \(R(X)\) is the ring of rational functions of \(X\), and consider the following following exact sequence, where \(K\) and \(C\) are defined as core and cokernel respectively: \[ 0 \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow i_* i^* F \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0 . \]

Here \(K\) and \(C\) are zero at each maximum point of \(X\), so according to (ii), \(\varphi(K)\) (resp. \(\varphi(C)\)) is strictly smaller than \(n = \varphi(F)\), hence \(H^q(X,K) = H^q(X,C) = 0\) for \(q \geqslant n\). So for demon- trer \(H^q(X,F) = 0\) for \(q > n\), it is enough, thanks to the exact sequences of cohomology, to demonstrate \(H^q(X,i_* i^* F) = 0\), so we can assume that \(F\) is of the form \(i_* G\), for a suitable \(\ell\)-beam \(G\) on \(\operatorname{Spec} R(X)\).
Let's examine the Leray spectral sequence. \[ E_2^{p,q} = H^p(X, R^q i_* G) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\operatorname{Spec} R(X), G) . \]

Or \(R(X)_{\text{réd}}\) is produced from the bodies \(k(x)\) for \(x\) maximum in \(X\), therefore \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) \leqslant n\) according to (i), therefore the outcome of the sequence spectral is zero in \(\dim > n\).

We want to demonstrate that \(E_2^{p0} = 0\) for \(p > n\). By examining the following spectral, we see that it is enough to demonstrate that \(E_2^{pq} = 0\) if \(q > 0\) and if \(p > n-q-1\). So, by induction, it suffices to demonstrate that \(\varphi(R^q i_* G) < n - q\) for \(q > 0\). But according to VIII 5.2 the fiber of \(R^q i_* G\) at a geometric point \(\bar{y}\) above a point \(y \in X\) is none other than \(H^q(\operatorname{Spec} K, G_K)\), where \(K\) is the ring of rational functions of the strict localization of \(X\) in \(\bar{y}\), and \(G_K\) is the induced beam on \(\operatorname{Spec} K\). We have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell K < n - \varphi(y)\) according to (ii), hence \((R^q i_* G)_{\bar{y}} = 0\) if \(q \geqslant n - \varphi(y)\), which gives the result searched.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.2

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian schema, \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\), \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and suppose that the following conditions are satisfied:

(i) For each point \(y\) of \(X\) of codimension \(c\), we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k(y) \leqslant n - 2c\).

(ii) For each ring \(A\), strict localization of a closed subschema irreducible \(Y\) of \(X\), we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) \leqslant \dim A\).

So \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell X \leqslant n . \]

More precisely, if \(F\) is a \(\ell\)-sheaf which is zero at every point \(y\) of codimension \(< s\), we have \[ H^q(X,F) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > n - 2s . \]

Indeed, we can take \(\varphi(y) = n-2\operatorname{codim} y\) and apply 4.1, whose condition (i) (resp. (ii)) results from the corresponding condition weight of (i) (resp. (ii)) of (4.2), taking into account for (ii) the ine- equality \(\dim \mathcal{O}_{Y,y} + \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,x} \leqslant \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,y}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

Let \(X\) be of finite type and dimension \(n\) on a body \(k\) and either \(\ell \neq \operatorname{car} k\). So \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell X \leqslant 2n + \operatorname{cd}_\ell k\).

We apply the previous corollary, replacing \(n\) with \(2n + \operatorname{cd}_\ell k\), and using 2.1 and 3.2 to check conditions (i) and (ii) of 4.2, and taking into account for (i) the inequality \[ \operatorname{deg.tr.} k(y)/k + \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,y} \leqslant \dim X . \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

Let \(X\) be Noetherian, and \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\) be invertible on \(X\). Suppose that condition (ii) of 4.2 is satisfied, and that furthermore \(\ell \neq 2\) where no residual body of \(X\) is orderable. So we have \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell X \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) + \dim X \leqslant 2\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) . \]

We set \(n = \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) + \dim X\) in 4.2, and we apply 2.4, which implies condition (i) of 4.2, because \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell k(y) \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(\mathcal{O}_{X,y}) - \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,y} \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) - \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,y} \leqslant n - 2 \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,y} . \] The second inequality in 4.4 follows from 2.5.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 4.5

The inelegant assumption about residual bodies made in 4.4, is necessary, as we see with \(X = \operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{R}[x,y,z]/(x^2+ y^2+ z^2)\), where all other conditions are satisfied, with \(\dim X = \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) = \ell = 2\), but the beam \(\mathbb{Z}/2\) concentrated at the point \((0,0,0)\) has a cohomology not zero in each dimension, therefore \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell X = + \infty\).

We are also tempted to try in 4.2 to put hypotheses only on closed points of \(X\), but this is not possible. By example there exist discrete valuation rings with residual bodies algebraically closed and with function fields having a cohomologous dimension arbitrarily large(∗). The spectrum of such a ring will also have a very large cohomological dimension.

footnotes.html: (∗) To be convinced of this, it suffices to note that if \(X\) is a smooth algebraic diagram on a field \(k\), admitting a point \(x \in X(k)\), then we can find a "formal arc of curve" passing through \(x\) whose "algebraic envelope" is \(X\), and whose body of functions therefore contains that of \(K\) of \(X\), and \(y\) induces a discrete valuation whose residual body is \(k\).

blocks.html: 5. Cohomological dimension: case \(\ell = p\)

Using the Artin - Schreier theory (IX 3.5) we obtain a better bound than 4.3 for the \(p\)-cohomological dimension of a schema \(X\) of characteristic \(p\). Let \(\operatorname{cdqc} X\) be the largest number \(n\) such as \(H^n(X,F) \neq 0\) for at least one bundle of Modules \(F\) quasi-consistent on \(X\) (in the Zariski sense). We have the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1

Let \(X\) be a Noetherian characteristic scheme \(p > 0\). So \[ \operatorname{cd}_p X \leqslant \operatorname{cdqc} X + 1 . \] In particular, the \(p\)-cohomological dimension of a Noetherian schema affine \(X\) with characteristic \(p > 0\) is at most equal to 1.

Demonstration. Let's apply IX 5.5: Taking into account the fact that the \(R^q f_* F\), \(q > 0\), are zero for a mor- finite phism \(f\) and for an abelian sheaf \(F\) (VIII 5.5) (resp. a quasi-coherent beam \(F\), we reduce ourselves to demonstrating that \(H^q(X,i_!(\mathbb{Z}/p)_U) = 0\) for \(i : U \longrightarrow X\) open immersion and for \(q > \operatorname{cdqc} X\).

Now let \(Y\) be a closed sub-preschema of \(X\) of support \(X - U\) and let \(J\) be the coherent bundle of ideals defining \(Y\). Morphism \(\wp : f \longmapsto f^p - f\) (IX 3.5) induces a morphism \(J \longrightarrow J\). By examining the exact sequel IX 3.5 and the exact sequel \[ 0 \longrightarrow i_!(\mathbb{Z}/p)_U \longrightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/p)_X \longrightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/p)_Y \longrightarrow 0 , \] we see that we actually have an exact sequence \[ (*) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow i_!(\mathbb{Z}/p)_U \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow 0 . \]
Since \(J\) is consistent we have \(H^q(X,J) = 0\) for \(q > \operatorname{cdqc} X\), hence the subsequently exact result of cohomology.
For a quasi-projective diagram on a body \(k\) separably closed, we can reduce this increase in certain cases by 1. We actually have

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2

Let \(X\) be a finite type schema on a body \(k\) of characteristic \(p > 0\). We have \[ \operatorname{cd}_p X \leqslant \dim X + 1 , \] and if \(k\) is separably closed and \(X\) quasi-projective(∗), we have \[ \operatorname{cd}_p X \leqslant \dim X . \]

Indeed, the first assertion is clear, since \(\operatorname{cdqc} X \leqslant \dim X\) ([6] 4.15.2). Let's deal with the second: For such a \(X\), the cohomology groups \(H^n(X,F)\) (\(n = \dim X\)), for a sheaf coherent \(F\), are finite-dimensional vector spaces on \(k\). Let \(V = H^n(X,J)\), where \(J\) is the bundle of ideals which appears in the exact sequence (∗). The morphism \(\varphi : V \longrightarrow V\) deduced from (∗) is obviously of the form \(\varphi = \varepsilon - \operatorname{id}\) where \(\varepsilon\) satisfies \(\varepsilon(rv) = r^p \varepsilon(v)\) for \(r \in k\) and \(v \in V\). The Jacobian of the application \(\Phi\) of the affine scheme deduced from \(\varphi\) is therefore \(-I\), \(I\) the identical matrix. As a result \(\Phi\) is spread, therefore surjective because additive, and it follows that \(\Phi\) is surjective since \(k\) is separably closed, hence immediately the conclusion.

footnotes.html: (∗) Using Chow's lemma, it is easy to replace the condition "\(X\) quasi-projective" by "\(X\) separated".

pages.title: 6. Cohomological dimension for a finite type prescheme on Spec \(\mathbb{Z}\)

blocks.html: 6. Cohomological dimension for a finite type prescheme on Spec \(\mathbb{Z}\)

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.1

Let \(X\) be quasi-finite above \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\). Suppose that \(\ell \neq 2\) or that each residual body of \(X\) of character- zero risk is totally imaginary. So \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell X \leqslant 3\).

This is a consequence of Theorem 2.2 (ii), of the Corollary 4.2 and the fact that a field of numbers (totally imaginary if \(\ell = 2\)) is of \(\ell\)-cohomological dimension \(\leqslant 2\) (CG II 4.4).
It actually follows from the theory of class bodies that we have equality if the morphism \(X \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\) is proper and surjective.

Unfortunately the results of No. 4 do not apply as which if \(X\) is only of finite type on \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\), because the hypo- thesis (ii) of (4.2) is not generally verified for local rings of residual characteristic \(\ell\). We must therefore use the result (5.1) to obtain the correct mark-up; we will now pay for the theorem (5.1).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.2

Let \(X\) be of finite type on \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\), and let \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\). Suppose, if \(\ell = 2\), that no residual body of \(X\) is orderable. So \[ \operatorname{cd}_\ell X \leqslant 2 \dim X + 1 . \] More precisely, let \(F\) be a \(\ell\)-torsion sheaf such that for all point \(x\) of \(X\) such that \(F_{\bar{x}} \neq 0\), we have \[ \dim \{\bar{x}\} \leqslant \begin{cases} (N-1)/2 & \text{si } \ell \neq \operatorname{car} k(x) \\ N-1 & \text{si } \ell = \operatorname{car} k(x) \end{cases} \] (\(\{\bar{x}\}\) is the stickiness of \(\{x\}\)); then \[ H^p(X,F) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad p > N . \]

Demonstration. By induction on \(X\), we can assume the theorem true for each closed sub-prescheme distinct from \(X\). If \(F\) is as in the statement and if we write \(F = \varinjlim F_i\) where the \(F_i\) are the constructible sub-sheaves of \(F\) (IX 2.9 (iii)), each \(F_i\) satisfies also the same assumptions. It is therefore sufficient to prove the theorem when \(F\) is constructible.

Or in this case the set \(E\) of \(x \in X\) such that \(F_{\bar{x}} \neq 0\) is a constructible subset, and the recurrence hypothesis implies that the theorem is true for each \(F\) such that \(E\) is not dense. Furthermore, the theorem is a consequence of 5.1 for a sheaf concentrated on the fiber at the point \((\ell)\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\), and 4.3 for a beam concentrated on the fiber in \((p)\), where \(p \neq \ell\), taking into account as \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z} = 1\) (CG II 2.2).

Let \(X\) be reduced, and let \(X = X_0 \cup X_1\) where \(X_1\) is the sub closed set, union of the irreducible components of \(X\) of characteristics ristic \(\ell\), and where \(X_0\) is the union of the other irreducible components tibles, \(X_0\) and \(X_1\) being provided with reduced induced structures. Let \(j_\nu : X_\nu \to X\) be the inclusions and let \(d_\nu = \dim X_\nu\), and \(N = \sup\{2d_0+1, d_1+1\}\). For all \(F\) constructible on \(X\) we have a exact sequence \[ (*) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow j_{0*} j_0^* F \oplus j_{1*} j_1^* F \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0 , \] where \(C\) is focused on \(X_0 \cap X_1\), which has characteristic \(\ell\) and of dimension \(\leqslant d_1-1\). It follows that \[ H^q(X,C) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > N - 1 , \] because by virtue of 5.1 we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell(X_0 \cap X_1) \leqslant \dim X_0 \cap X_1 + 1 \leqslant d_1 \leqslant N-1\). By examining the exact relative cohomology sequence of (∗), we brings back to the case \(X = X_0\) where \(X = X_1\), and the latter is a consequence of 5.1, as already reported.

Let us therefore suppose that each maximum point of \(X\) is of character- ristic different from \(\ell\), and be \(i_\nu : x_\nu \longrightarrow X\) the inclusions of maximum points. By examining the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow \bigoplus_\nu i_{\nu*} i_\nu^* F \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0 \] we reduce by induction to the case \(F = i_{\nu*} i_\nu^* F\), that is to say \(F\) of the form \(i_* G\) for a suitable beam \(G\) on \(\operatorname{Spec} k(x)\), \(x\) a point of \(X\). We can therefore assume \(X\) irreducible and reduced, to the point generic \(x\), and that the characteristic of \(k(x)\) is zero according to 4.3.
Let's examine the spectral sequence \[ (**) \qquad H^p(X, R^q i_* G) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\operatorname{Spec} k(x), G) . \]
Let \(s = \dim X\). We must demonstrate that \[ (+) \qquad E_2^{p0} = 0 \quad \text{pour } p > 2s + 1 . \]

Or \(k(x)[i]\) is of degree of transcendence \(s-1\) above \(\mathbb{Q}[i]\) which is a field of cohomological dimension 2. Since \(k(x)\) is not orderable if \(\ell = 2\), it follows from 2.1 that we have \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell k(x) = s + 1\). This implies that the goal of the spectral sequence is zero for \(n > s + 1\). It is therefore sufficient for (+) to demonstrate that in (∗∗) we have \[ (++) \qquad E_2^{pq} = 0 \quad \text{si } p > 2s - q \quad \text{et } q > 0 . \]

Let \(y\) be a point of \(X\) and \(K\) be the ring of fractions of a strictly localized at a geometric point \(\tilde{y}\) of \(X\) above \(y\). Then \(K\) is a direct product of bodies each of which has a degree of transcendence \(s-1\) on a strict localization of \(\mathbb{Z}\), and we therefore have, according to 2.1 and 2.2, \(\operatorname{cd}_\ell K \leqslant s\). Furthermore we can apply 3.2 if the residual characteristic of \(y\) is not \(\ell\). We therefore have

(") \[ (R^q i_* G)_{\tilde{y}} = 0 \begin{cases} \text{si } q > \operatorname{codim} y, \text{ i.e. } \dim \bar{y} > s-q, \text{ et } \ell \neq \operatorname{car} k(y), \\ \text{ou si } q > s \text{ en tout cas.} \end{cases} \]
In particular we have \(E_2^{pq} = 0\) if \(q > s\). To check (++) in the case \(0 < q \leqslant s\), we apply the recurrence hypothesis to the sheaves \(R^q i_*(G)\).

According to the statement and ("), it is necessary to set \(N' = 2s - q\) and check the two inequalities \[ s - q \leqslant (N'-1)/2 \] \[ s-1 = \dim X_\ell \leqslant N'-1 , \] where \(X_\ell\) is the bundle of \(X\) above the point \((\ell)\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\). They are indeed true if \(0 < q \leqslant s\), cqfd.
Bibliography
[1] AX, J. Proof of some conjectures of Serre on cohomological dimension, Proc. Bitter. Math. Society 16 (1965) 1214-1221.
[2] BOURBAKI, N. Commutative Algebra, Paris (1960).
[3] HIRONAKA, H. Resolution of singularities of an algebraic surface over a field of characteristic zero, Annals of Math. Flight. 79, No. 1,2 (1964).
[4] NAGATA, M. Local Rings, New York (1962).
[5] SERRE, J.-P. On the cohomological dimension of groups profinis, Topology Vol. 3, p. 413 (1965).
[6] GODEMENT, R. Theory of Beams, Paris (1958).
[7] E. ARTIN and A. SCHREIER — Eine Kennzeichnung der reell abgeschlossenen Körper, Hamb. Abh. 5 (1927) pp. 225-231.
[8] SERRE, J.P. Galoisian Cohomology, Lecture Notes n°5 (Springer).
